messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about VSF Behavior?

VSF Behavior: Each stack member must have a unique member ID number. Auto-stacking automatically assigns the lowest available member ID when adding a new member to the stack; if deploying or expanding a stack manually, ensure that each new member is assigned a valid member ID not already in use by an existing stack member, as a member ID conflict will result in the new member failing to join the stack. • During normal stack operation, the primary member will assume the Conductor role and the secondary member will assume the Standby role during normal operation. • The primary member is member number 1. This setting is not configurable and 1 is the default. A factory-default switch boots up as a VSF-enabled switch with a member number of 1. • The secondary member number is user configurable; when auto-stacking is used via the push-button or CLI methods, member 2 is automatically assigned as the secondary. It is recommended that the customer configures a secondary member in the stack, since a stack with a standby offers resiliency and high availability.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Management interface?

Management interface: In a VSF stack, only the management interface on the Conductor switch will be assigned an IP address (configured or assigned by DHCP). The stack allows connectivity to management protocols and Console through the management interface on the Conductor. NOTE The management interface is applicable only for 6200 and 6300 devices. Public Consoles 36 Split detection 6200, 6300 and 6300L Switch Series On 6200, 6300 and 6300L switch series VSF stack supports split detection utilizing the management interfaces, which requires users to connect the management interfaces of the primary and secondary stack members to the same L2 network. NOTE It is also possible to connect the management interfaces of primary and secondary directly to one another for split detection.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Features that don't support uninterrupted operation during ISSU?

Features that don't support uninterrupted operation during ISSU: Hardware faults It is recommended that hardware faults are resolved prior to performing ISSU. Fan faults and abnormal temperature statuses should be addressed. Fans may increase in speed during the ISSU process. Public VSF ISSU considerations 55 Prohibited operations During ISSU the following operations are not permitted: • Any hardware hot swap • Pressing any button Performing any of these operations during an ISSU could put the system in an error state that could adversely affect the ISSU. The system may not be able to address failures during the ISSU. For example, if network protocols require blocking a port, flush MAC, or routing tables, etc. the switch would not be able to react to those tasks until the ISSU is complete. Recommendations Before initiating ISSU, it is recommended to enable spanning-tree root-guard on the supposed designated ports of the VSF stack. This ensures and enforces the root bridge placement in the network.

topic: Features that don't support uninterrupted operation during ISSU

For Restrictions, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: AAA Procedure 1. During ISSU new client authentication will occur on other members except the VSF conductor that is rebooting. Clients will not be able to access to the network until ISSU is complete. 2. Session timeout/inactivity timeout applied to clients will be delayed. Clients will remain longer than the configured timer. PIM with MSDP Currently, PIM is not supported in ISSU on RP routers, therefore traffic loss is expected during ISSU on RP routers where PIM and MSDP are enabled. Public Show tech 57 F e a t u r e s t h a t d o n ' t s u p p o r t u n i n t e r r u p t e d o p e r a t i o... Dynautz ISSU All Dynamic Authorization requests will be rejected with NAK during ISSU operation. Captive portal workflow will be affected due to NAK received for Dynauth requests during ISSU. BFD ISSU It's important to note that this mechanism is only recommended for sessions that have a failure detection time higher than 6 seconds (e.g. Tx=Rx=3000 and Detect Multiplier=2, or Tx=Rx=2000 and Detect Multiplier=3).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Feature readiness check?

Feature readiness check: ISSU and feature readiness check will fail if the feature ready check does not pass. The feature ready check validates that none of the following features are in a failed state: • ACLs, object groups, and their applications • Classifier policies, classes, and their applications • Port-access polices, classes, and their applications • Port-access group-based policies, classes, and their applications • Active PBR actions and their applications In general, if any of the above is in a failed state it is recommended to fix it and then upgrade to the newer operating system image using the system reboot method instead of ISSU. Some failed states will remain even after it is fixed on a running system and a reboot is good best practice for clearing it.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the issu update-software [validate|confirm] command do?

The issu update-software [validate|confirm] command initiates ISSU to the alternate boot location. The newer operating system image must be downloaded to the alternate boot location prior to running this command. Additionally, the current running operating system version must match the version that is stored in the current boot location or ISSU will not be allowed. CAUTION This operation is disruptive and will result in the management interface being disconnected during the process. Starting an ISSU will cause the running configuration to be saved in case an error is encountered that requires a reboot to recover the switch. In addition, a special configuration checkpoint will be stored to disk and is used to roll back to the pre-ISSU firmware. Syntax: issu update-software [validate|confirm].

The type <TYPE> command configures the part number of the VSF member being provisioned. After provisioning, the interfaces of the member are available for configuration. When the member eventually joins the stack, it will boot up with the configuration made on the pre- provisioned interfaces. To provision a member, the member number and the part number of the member must be specified. The no form of this command removes the configuration for the part number of the VSF member provisioned. messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show issu command do?

The show issu command shows information about the current state of ISSU. If no ISSU is currently in progress, the command displays the progress details of the last ISSU. The command with the brief parameter displays a short summary of the ISSU state and indicates if the system is ready to accept an ISSU command and whether or not an ISSU is in progress. If the brief parameter is not included, then more details about an in progress ISSU or the last ISSU are displayed. Shows a short summary of the ISSU state. Shows details of ISSU software update history. Shows information about the current state of an ISSU validation. Syntax: show issu [brief|history|validation].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsf egress-shape command do?

The vsf egress-shape command when the VSF links are of different interface speeds, the command ensures that all VSF interfaces in a stack operate at a chosen common denominator interface speed when there is at least one active stack member of type S0E91A or S0X44A in the stack. The no form of this command removes the common denominator speed from all VSF links and ensure that all the VSF links operate at their original speed. NOTE VSF egress-shape is enabled by default on the switch. This feature is applicable only for 6300 switch series (except for S3L75A, S3L76A and S3L77A). Enables egress shaping on all VSF interfaces. Syntax: vsf egress-shape.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsf split-detect command do?

The vsf split-detect command configures the VSF split detection method that specifies the mechanism used for stack fragment discovery when there is a VSF stack split. Once the stack fragments are discovered, the fragment having the primary member always takes precedence. All non-VSF interfaces on the secondary stack fragment will be brought down to minimize network disruption due to duplicate MAC/IP addresses. Configures a management interface as the split detection meth od on 6200 and 6300 Switch series. The management interfac es of the primary and secondary members should be either con nected to the same L2 network or directly connected to each ot her. Negates any configured parameter and removes the VSF split d etection configuration. mgmt no. Syntax: vsf split-detect.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsf member <MEMBER-ID> command do?

The vsf member <MEMBER-ID> command creates VSF member context in the switch for the specified member. The no form of this command removes the specified member from the stack. All configuration associated with the member, as well as the subsystems and interfaces of the member will also be removed. If the member is physically present in the stack at the time it is removed, it will reboot with the default configuration and lose its identity as a member of the stack from which it was removed. NOTE When a physically present member is removed, it may cause the stack to split. VSF member identifier. • Range for 6200F devices: 1 to 8. • Range for 6300 devices: 1 to 10. Syntax: vsf member <MEMBER-ID>.

Configuration conflict finder recommendations: NOTE Configuration Conflict Finder (CCF) is supported only on 6200 and 6300 platforms. Configuration Conflict Finder(CCF) is a configuration troubleshooting solution that allows admins and support to automatically determine switch configuration anomalies using a set of feature configuration templates. This ensures specific features are error-free and validates the switch's configuration. CCF is an especially useful tool for CX users, support personnel, and ERT. Public.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about How do I configure split-detect using MAD VLAN? ?

How do I configure split-detect using MAD VLAN? : The 4100i and 6100 platforms support split-detection using a dedicated Multi-Active Detection (MAD) VLAN and front-plane ports. Public General 127 To enable split-detection: • Physical Connection: Connect one of the front-plane ports of the primary member to one of the front-plane ports of the secondary member. Ensure both ports are either: Connected to the same management VLAN/network, or Directly connected to each other. • VLAN Configuration: Reserve a dedicated VLAN (e.g., VLAN ID 100) for split-detection.

Split Detection VLAN interfaces: 1/1/1, 2/1/1: Split Detection Operational status: Up NOTE Replace interface numbers and VLAN ID as appropriate for your deployment. How do I remove the non-VSF configurations in a stack? Use the erase startup-config command on the VSF stack. This action will remove all non-VSF related configurations from the startup-config. Then reboot the stack. Public General 128 Can a VSF member be removed from a stack? Yes, remove a member from the stack using the no vsf member <MEMBER-ID> command. All configurations associated with the member will also be removed. The member will boot and come back up with the factory default configuration. How do I remove the conductor switch from the stack? HPE Aruba Networking does not recommend removing a member that is conductor of a stack. If the conductor switch has to be removed, complete a switchover and wait for: • the standby to take up the conductor role, and • the old conductor to reboot and join the stack as standby.

D>|all] IPv6 <X: X: : X: X>|all vrf (Optional) Include an IP address to clear neighbor entries for th at specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries for all IP addresses. (Optional) Include an IPv6 address to clear neighbor entries for that specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries fo r all IPv6 addresses. Clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries for the specified VRF or for all VRFs. If no VRF is specified he default VRF is cleared. all-vrfs Clear neighbor entries for all VRFs Clear neighbor entries for the specified VRF. Syntax: clear arp.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show arp state command do?

The show arp state command shows ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) cache entries that are in the specified state. Shows the ARP cache entries for all VRF (Virtual Router Forwa rding) instances. Shows the ARP cache entries that are in failed state. The n eighbor might have been deleted. incomplete Shows the ARP cache entries that are in incomplete state. permanent reachable An incomplete state means that address resolution is in progres s and the link-layer address of the neighbor has not yet bee n determined. A solicitation request was sent, and the switch is waiting for a solicitation reply or a timeout. Shows the ARP cache entries that are in permanent state. ARP entries that are in a permanent state can be removed by a dministrative action only. Syntax: show arp state {all | failed | incomplete | permanent | reachable | stale}.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command changes the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current subnet. The router uses hello packets to inform neighbor routers of its presence and to comput the hello hold time. Hello hold time informs neighbor routers estimated wait time for the next hello packet from the router. Shortening the hello interval reduces the hello hold time. Default is set to 30 seconds. The no form of this command resets the frequency of PIM hello messages back to the default value on the current subnet. Public ip pim-bidir hello-interval 297 Configures the given value as hello interval. Range: 1-300 seco nds. • The default value (30 seconds) covers most deployment needs; change only for explicit convergence or timing requirements. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. In chatty networks where there is a chance of Hello packets being lost, configuring a higher hold time reduces the likelihood of adjacency loss. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command do?

The IPv6 pim6-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. In chatty networks where there is a chance of Hello packets being lost, configuring a higher hold time reduces the likelihood of adjacency loss. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay command enables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. With lan-prune-delay enabled, the router informs downstream neighbors on the wait time before pruning a flow after receiving a prune request. The no form of this command disables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-auth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-auth command use this command to allow or block authentication on the CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit). This is allowed by default. The no form of this command prevents authentication on CDP packets received on the port. This command can be issued from the interface (config-if) or Link Aggregation Group (config-lag-if) contexts. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-auth.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu command do?

The aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu command allows all packets related to the CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) on a secure port or LAG. This command can be issued from the interface (config-if) or Link Aggregation Group (config-lag-if) contexts. The no form of this command blocks the CDP BPDU on a secure port. On a nonsecure port, the command has no effect. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-proxy-logoff command do?

The aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-proxy-logoff command allows a client to be logged off from the system via a special TLV in the CDP packet. By default, proxy logoff via CDP packet support is disabled. When allow-cdp-proxy-logoff is enabled, TLV received from CDP packets corresponding to logoff processing will be read and logoff is issued to the clients. This only works on client authentication enabled ports and aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu must be enabled to process. This command can be issued from the interface (config-if) or Link Aggregation Group (config-lag-if) contexts. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-proxy-logoff.

Default: default. Syntax: no ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> hold-off-interval command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> hold-off-interval command specifies hold-off interval in units of 100 ms. If specified, a defect is not reported immediately. Instead, the hold-off timer is started. On expiration of the timer, if the defect still exists, it is reported to protection switching. The default value for hold-off timer is 0. The no form of this command removes the configured value of the hold-off interval and sets it to the default value of 0. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the hold-off interval in units of 100 ms. De fault: 0. <RINGID> <100 milliseconds> On a common ERPS network, a physical ring can be configured with a single ERPS ring, and only one blocked port can be specified in the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> control-vlan command this command adds a control-channel VLAN to a ring instance. In an ERPS ring, the control VLANshould be used only to forward RAPS PDUs and not service packets. All the devices in an ERPS ring instance must be configured with the same control VLAN, and different ERPS ring instances must use different control VLANs. The no form of this command removes the control-channel VLAN of the ring instance. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> <VID> This command adds descriptive information to help administrators and operators understand the purpose of a ring instance. 1-64 printable ASCII characters are allowed. The no form of this command removes the ring instance description. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command configures the default revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In revertive operation, after the conditions causing protection switching are cleared, traffic channels are restored to the recovered link blocking the RPL. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. The no form of this command configures non-revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In non-revertive operation, the traffic channels continue to use the RPL, if it has not failed, after conditions causing protection switching are cleared. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command the R-APS messages transmitted by ERPS take the form of OAM PDUs as defined in G.8013. Each OAM PDU is transmitted at a specified level known as the Maintenance Entity Group (MEG) level. This command configures the level with which the ERPS packets must be transmitted. The no form of this command removes the configured MEG level and sets it to the default value of 7. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the meg-level. Range: 0-7. Default: 7. <RINGID> <0-7> This command associates a sub-ring to a parent-ring and is required for the sub-ring to notify the parent- ring on change in topology. The no form of this command removes the parent ring identifier. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip icmp unreachable command do?

The ip icmp unreachable command enables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages on the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. The unreachable host address originates from the failed packed. Default setting. The no form of this command disables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages from the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. This command does not prevent other hosts from sending an ICMP unreachable message. Syntax: no ip icmp unreachable.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp router-alert-check command do?

The ip igmp router-alert-check command enables or disables IGMP router alert check for IGMP packets. IGMP packets without the router alert field set are dropped if router alert check is enabled. Router alert check is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables router alert check for IGMP packets. Enable IGMP router alert check. Disable IGMP router alert check. messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command do?

The ip igmp snooping all-vlans command enable IGMP snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables IGMP snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command is issued. Public ip igmp snooping all-vlans 1035 When IGMP snooping is enabled globally, IGMP snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global IGMP snooping, allows IGMP snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level IGMP snooping settings. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping all-vlans.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip tcp mss <VALUE> command do?

The ip tcp mss <VALUE> command sets the TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) MSS (Maximum Segment Size) for an interface. When configured, the MSS option in the TCP header is set in the TCP handshake for negotiation during connection establishment. Once the TCP session is established, changing the TCP MSS value on the interface will not affect the existing session. The no form of this command removes the TCP MSS configuration from the interface. NOTE TCP MSS is supported on ROP, SVI, subinterface, and L3 GRE tunnel interface. Specifies the IPv4 TCP MSS in bytes. Range: 68 to 65495. <VALUE>. Syntax: no ip tcp mss <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command do?

The ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command enables the IPv6-only-preferred mode on the VLAN interface, and is also supported on the OOBM interface. Once enabled, the DHCP client prioritizes a IPv6 address over an IPv4 address. To obtain a DHCPv4 address, the IPv6-only-preferred command must be disabled, and the DHCPv4 client must be restarted. This command is disabled by default. NOTE Ensure the server supports the IPv6-only-preferred mode option, as the command will not work unless supported by the server. The no form of this command disables IPv6-only-preferred mode. NOTE The IPv6-only-preferred mode command must be configured prior to the ip dhcp command, otherwise it will not take effect. n/a Public ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred 1175. Syntax: no ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra max-interval command configures the maximum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the default lifetime value. Specifies the maximum advertisement time in seconds. Range: 4-1800. Default: 600 seconds. <TIME> Configures the minimum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra max-interval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command specifies the maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives. When a new advertisement arrives, hold time is reset. Hold time must be greater than or equal to the maximum advertisement interval. Therefore, if the hold time for an advertisement expires, the host can reasonably conclude that the router interface that sent the advertisement is no longer available. The default hold time is three times the maximum advertisement interval. The no form of this command removes the specified maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives and update it to the default value. Specifies the lifetime of router advertisements sent from this in terface. Range: 4 to 9000 seconds. Syntax: no ip irdp holdtime <TIME>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command specifies the minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements. By default, this value is automatically set by the switch to be 75% of the value configured for maximum router advertisement interval. Use this command to override the automatically configured value. The no form of this command removes the specified minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements and reverts to the default value. Specifies the minimum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 3 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 450 seconds (75% of the default value for maximum route r advertisement interval). <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface persona lag <NAME> command do?

The interface persona lag <NAME> command creates a persona LAG, a template for LAG (Link Aggregation Group) interfaces. A persona LAG defines configuration options that can be applied to multiple LAGs for consistency and simplified management. Only LAG interfaces can reference persona LAG templates. The persona LAG name cannot use reserved interface names or formats, such as vlan, lag, or loopback. Configurations defined in a persona LAG are valid only for LAG interfaces. The no form of this command deletes the persona LAG interface. Specifies the name of the persona LAG. <NAME>. Syntax: no interface persona lag <NAME>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command do?

The aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command enables/disables privilege elevation for the users in an administrators group. When enabled, an administrative user is provided lower privilege access upon successful switch login. The user must execute an enable command and re-authenticate to elevate their privilege to administrator (the administrator level Public aaa authentication login privilege-elevation 1386 access with ‘#’ prompt). Executing the disable command revokes an administrator privilege and returns the user to the lower privilege access level (the operator level access ‘>’ prompt). The no form of this command disables privilege elevation for the administrator user group. Syntax: no aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command do?

The aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command defines authorization as being basic local RBAC (specified as none), or as full-fledged local RBAC specified as local (the default), or as remote TACACS+ (specified with group <GROUP-LIST>). Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually. All server groups named in the command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. The no form of this command unconfigures authorization for the specified connection type, reverting to the default of local. NOTE Although only TACACS+ servers are supported for remote authorization, local authorization (basic or full-fledged) can be used with remote RADIUS authentication. Syntax: no aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping command this command configures the drop unknown mode. While MLD snooping is enabled, the traffic will be forwarded only to ports that initiate an MLD request for multicast. Drop unknown mode can be a filter across all VLANs (vlan-shared) or per VLAN (exclusive-vlan). The default configuration is vlan-shared. The no form of this command configures the drop unknown mode on the switch to the default vlan-shared. Required: Enable shared VLAN filter on the switch. vlan-exclusive Required: Enable exclusive drop unknown filter per VLAN. Public MLD snooping global configuration commands 1554 This command delays the refresh for some IGMP or MLD protocol-related values. messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command enable MLD snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables MLD snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command is issued. When MLD snooping is enabled globally, MLD snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global MLD snooping, allows MLD snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level MLD snooping settings. Syntax:
- no IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans
- IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans 1561.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the specified ports as fastleave ports. Enables the switch to immediately remove an interface from the bridge table upon receiving the leave group message. The no form of this command disables fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). MLD fastleave is configured for ports on a per-VLAN basis. By default, the querier sends a MLD Group- Specific Query message out of the interface, upon which the leave group message is received to ensure that no other receivers are connected to the interface. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [forward vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [forward vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command by default ports are configured in auto mode. This command configures the given ports in forward mode. The no form of this command disables forward ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a forward port. Specifies the number of a single V LAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 6 0). This command configures the hardware packet forwarding feature for MLD control packets on MLD snooping enabled VLANs. When this feature is enabled, MLD control packets are forwarded in the hardware to the filter additive ports instead of software reforwarding so that all the switches in a L2 cascaded network will learn the control packets at the same time. Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping hardware-packet-forwarding [enable | disable].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command this command enables AOS-CX leaf devices (access-layer switches that connect directly to servers, storage, and other endpoints) to unconditionally forward multicast traffic to all VTEPs that do not support the exchange of MLD reports over the fabric. By default, when MLD snooping is enabled in AOS-CX, multicast traffic is selectively forwarded only to VTEPs connected to have interested hosts. This is achieved by exhanging MLD reports over VXLAN tunnels where each VTEP processes the remote reports and uses the membership information to forward multicast traffic efficiently, conserving fabric bandwidth. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip ospf retransmit-interval command do?

The ip ospf retransmit-interval command sets the time between retransmitting lost link state advertisements for the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the time between retransmitting lost link state advertisements to the default of 5 seconds for the OSPF interface. Specifies the retransmit interval, in seconds. Range: 1 to 3600. Default: 5. <INTERVAL> Sets the SHA (secure hash authentication) key for the selected interface. If the SHA key is given in ciphertext, it will be decrypted and applied to the protocol. This command accepts a key of up to 64 characters irrespective of the SHA version configured on the interface. OSPF will internally pad zeros to the key to obtain a 64-byte key. For all types of SHA, key length is adjusted to 64 bytes. The no form of this command deletes the SHA authentication key. Syntax: ip ospf retransmit-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command configures a static OSPFv3 neighbor on the interface for point-to-multipoint networks. Static neighbors are required on non-broadcast networks where automatic neighbor discovery using multicast hello packets is not possible. The neighbor IPv6 address must be the link-local IPv6 address of the neighboring router on the same link. This command is valid only on interfaces configured with the point-to-multipoint network type. It is required for non-broadcast point-to-multipoint networks. The no version of this command removes the static neighbor configuration from the interface. Public IPv6 ospfv3 neighbor 1913 neighbor <neighbor-IPv6- Static neighbor IPv6 link-local address. address>. Syntax: no IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>}.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command runs the OSPFv3 protocol on the interface for the area specified. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol Specifies the area ID is one of the following formats. • Area ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal num ber from 0 to 255. • Area ID as a decimal value. Range: 0-4294967295. Specifies the OSPFv3 process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. <AREA-ID> <PROCESS-ID> Configures null authentication on an interface which disables IPsec authentication. Configures IPSec AH authentication. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-dense hello-interval command do?

The ip pim-dense hello-interval command configures the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 30 seconds. Required: Specifies the frequency at which PIM Hello messages are transmitted on this interface. Default: 30 seconds. Range: 1 -300 seconds. Public ip pim-dense hello-interval 2013 NOTE • The default value (30 seconds) covers most deployment needs; change only for explicit convergence or timing requirements. • Neighbor Hold Time is derived automatically as 3.5 × hello-interval; reducing the interval tightens failure detection windows and heightens sensitivity to transient loss. • Extremely low intervals can cause neighbor flaps if a few Hellos are lost during congestion or microbursts. Syntax: ip pim-dense hello-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command do?

The ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. If not configured, the implementation derives the value from the current hello-interval. Syntax: ip pim-dense hello-holdtime <holdtime-VALUE>.

The show ip mroute (PIM-DM) command shows multicast routing information. Optionally, you can show specific information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Shows mroute information for all VRFs. Optional. Shows mroute information for a particular VRF. If the <VRF-N AME> is not specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Optional. Shows the multicast routing information for the given group address. Optionally, you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. <GROUP-ADDR> <SOURCE-ADDR> Specifies a group address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies show information for the group from this source in IP v 4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Syntax: show ip mroute [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense max-graft-retries command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense max-graft-retries command configures the number of attempts the routing switch will retry sending the same graft packet to join a flow. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 3 attempts. Specifies the number of retries for the routing switch to resend the graft packet. Default: 3 attempts. Range: 1-10. <INTERVAL-VALUE> Configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets the value to the default of 2500 ms. Specifies the override interval of a LAN Prune Delay option in m s. Default: 2500 ms. Range: 500-6000. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense max-graft-retries <ATTEMPT-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense ttl-threshold command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense ttl-threshold command configures the multicast datagram time-to-live (router hop-count) threshold for the interface. Any IP multicast datagrams or state-refresh packets with a TTL less than this threshold will not be forwarded out the interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 3 attempts. Specifies the time-to-live threshold. Default: 3 attempts. Ran ge: 0-255. <THRESHOLD-VALUE> Removes PIM-DM for all IPv6 related configurations for the interface. Specifies the source interface to be used for PIM registration in the case of VXLAN anycast interfaces. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense ttl-threshold <THRESHOLD-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-sparse accept-remote-source command do?

The ip pim-sparse accept-remote-source command enables the router to accept multicast traffic from remote sources. When enabled, the router accepts multicast traffic from sources that are not directly connected and acts as the First Hop Router (FHR), performing registration to the Rendezvous Point (RP). In scenarios where a Gateway BD is configured in EVPN OISM networks, this should be applied to the external router interface that connects to the Gateway BD. The no form of this command removes the configuration on the interface and sets it to the default configuration. Syntax: no ip pim-sparse accept-remote-source.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command enables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. The default is enabled. With LAN-prune-delay enabled, the router informs downstream neighbors how long it will wait before pruning a flow after receiving a prune request. Other downstream routers on the same interface must send a join to override the prune before the LAN-prune-delay time to continue the flow. Prompts any downstream neighbors with multicast receivers continuing to belong to the flow to reply with a join. If no joins are received after the LAN-prune-delay period, the router prunes the flow. The propagation-delay and override-interval settings determine the LAN-prune-delay setting. Public IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay 2219 The no form of this command disables the LAN prune delay option. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command configures an application to associate with a user-configured certificate. By default, all applications are associated with the default certificate "local-cert". Note that applications "captive-portal" and "hsc" are only available on certain platforms. The certificate associated with EST client is global to all EST profiles. Among the available applications, only dot1x-supplicant, est-client, and radsec-client are allowed to associate with device-identity certificate. Also note that device-identity certificate on a switch using a TPM of legacy version 1.2 only works with TLS version 1.2. 1. Syntax: no crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command creates a leaf certificate and changes to its context config-cert-<CERT-NAME>. If the specified leaf certificate exists, this command changes to its context. The first time the switch starts it creates a self-signed, default leaf certificate called local-cert. This certificate is used by any switch application that does not have an associated leaf certificate. The no form of this command deletes the specified leaf certificate. The default leaf certificate local-cert cannot be deleted. Public crypto pki certificate 2326 Specifies the name of a leaf certificate. Range: 1 to 32 alphanu meric characters (excluding "). <CERT-NAME>. Syntax: no crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>.

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator cached-reauth command enables cached reauthentication on a port. Cached reauthentication allows 802.1X reauthentications to succeed when the RADIUS server is unavailable. Users already authenticated retain their currently assigned RADIUS attributes. messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the qos queue-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos queue-profile <NAME> command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. Each profile has one to eight queues numbered 0 to 7. The larger the queue number, the higher its priority during transmission scheduling. A queue profile named factory-default is defined by default and is automatically applied to all interfaces. It cannot be edited or deleted. Use the command show qos queue-profile factory-default to view this profile. Do not use show running-configuration, as it will only display changes from the initial values. Syntax: no qos queue-profile <NAME>.

The qos schedule-profile <NAME> command creates a QoS schedule profile and switches to the config-schedule context for the profile. If the specified schedule profile exists, this command switches to the config-schedule context for the profile. The schedule profile determines the order in which queues are selected to transmit a packet, and the amount of service defined for each queue. Specifies the name of the QoS queue profile to create or config ure. Range: 1 to 64 alphanumeric characters, including period (.), underscore (_), and hyphen (-). <NAME>. Syntax: no qos schedule-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the qos shape command do?

The qos shape command limits the egress bandwidth on an interface to a value that is lower than its line rate. Errors will be generated in the following events: • A user configures a port-shaping value that is greater than 100 percent • A user configures a kbps port-shaping value that is less than the supported minimum kbps value. The supported minimum kbps shaping value can be retrieved using the show capacities command. The no form of this command removes shaping from an interface. Specifies the maximum traffic rate in kbps. Range: 1 to 10000 0000. Alternatively, the bandwidth can also be configured as a percentage of link bandwidth. The supported range is 1-100. Default units are kilobits per second. Syntax: qos shape <RATE> [kbps|percent].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the no aaa accounting port-access command do?

The no aaa accounting port-access command configures port access accounting information that is captured for 802.1X and MAC-authenticated clients. Defines port access accounting as being local (with the parameter local) (the default). Or defines port access accounting as being remote (with the parameter group <GROUP-NAME>) with a sequence of remote RADIUS servers in a single RADIUS server group to be accessed for port access accounting purposes. For remote RADIUS port access accounting, the information is sent to the first reachable remote RADIUS server in the specified group. If a user-defined RADIUS server group is named in your command, it must exist. The no form of this command works as follows: • no aaa accounting port-access: Globally unconfigures port access accounting. Syntax:
- no aaa accounting port-access local
- no aaa accounting port-access group
- no aaa accounting port-access interim.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the radius-server timeout [<1-60>] command do?

The radius-server timeout [<1-60>] command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the RADIUS server before trying the next RADIUS server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the RADIUS global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Specifies the timeout interval of 1 to 60 seconds. Default: 5 sec onds. Syntax: no radius-server timeout [<1-60>].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the tacacs-server key command do?

The tacacs-server key command creates or modifies a TACACS+ global passkey. The TACACS+ global passkey is used as a shared-secret for encrypting the communication between all TACACS+ servers and the switch. The TACACS+ global passkey is required for authentication unless local passkeys have been set. By default, the TACACS+ global passkey is empty. If the administrator has not set this key, the switch will not be able to perform TACACS+ authentication. The switch will instead rely on the authentication mechanism configured with aaa authentication login. NOTE When this command is entered without parameters, plaintext passkey prompting occurs upon pressing Enter. The entered passkey characters are masked with asterisks. The no form of the command removes the global passkey. Syntax: tacacs-server key [plaintext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY> | ciphertext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY>].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>] command do?

The snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>] command associates an SNMP MIB view with the SNMP community. The no form of this command removes the associated SNMP MIB view from the SNMP community. Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Specifies the view name for the SNMP MIB view. Accepts a maxi mum of 32 characters. <STRING> <VIEW-NAME>. Syntax: no snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server community <STRING> command do?

The snmp-server community <STRING> command adds an SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. A community string is like a password that controls read/ write access to the SNMP agent. A network management program must supply this name when attempting to get SNMP information from the switch. A maximum of 10 community strings are supported. Once you create your own community string, the default community string (public) is deleted. The no form of this command removes the specified SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. When no community string exists, a default community string with the value public is automatically defined. <STRING> Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Syntax: no snmp-server community <STRING>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server snmpv3-only command do?

The snmp-server snmpv3-only command accepts SNMP v3 messages only, SNMP v1 and SNMP v2c will be disabled. By default SNMP v1, SNMP v2c and SNMP v3 will all be enabled. The no form of this command restores the default setting and reenables SNMP v1 and SNMP v2c. Syntax: no snmp-server snmpv3-only.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server trap aaa-server-reachability-status command do?

The snmp-server trap aaa-server-reachability-status command enables the SNMP trap for AAA server status. When enabled, traps are sent whenever AAA server (RADIUS, TACACS) status changes from reachable to unreachable and vice versa. The no form of this command disables sending SNMP trap for AAA server status. Syntax: no snmp-server trap aaa-server-reachability-status.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server trap mac-notify command do?

The snmp-server trap mac-notify command enables the MAC notification traps within the SNMP module at a global level. When enabled, traps are sent for interfaces that are configured for MAC notification events. The no form of this command disables sending MAC notification traps at a global level. When disabled, existing mac-notify interface configuration is preserved but MAC notification events on configured interfaces will not cause SNMP traps to be transmitted. Syntax: no snmp-server trap mac-notify.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show environment power-consumption command do?

The show environment power-consumption command displays power consumption information. Displays detailed power consumption information. For VSF supported platforms only. Displays the power consum ption information for the specified VSF member. Range: 1-10. Power consumed values are updated every 5 seconds. The total power consumed is the total power used in a chassis. The power consumed average is calculated from the total power consumed as a running average over a period of time. The average period has a default of 10 minutes. The period can be configured using power-consumption-average-period. On chassis where power input is not supported, power consumed is displayed as Power Usage and PSU Output Power is displayed as N/A. Syntax: show environment power-consumption [detail].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip errors command do?

The show ip errors command shows harware and software IP error statistics for packets received by the switch since the switch was last booted. If none of the optoinal VRF parameters are included, the show ip errors command displays the current system-wide hardware IP error counters and the default VRF's software IP error counters on the switch Optional. Display hardware and software IP error counters for a specific VRF. Optional. Display hardware and software IP error counters for al - VRFs IP error info about received packets is collected from each active line card on the switch and is preserved during failover events. Error counts are cleared when the switch is rebooted. Syntax: show ip errors [{vrf <vrf-name>}|all-vrfs].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ssh certificate-as-authorized-key command do?

The ssh certificate-as-authorized-key command enables SSH enforcement that the username must be present in the certificate that is being used for authorization. This configuration alters how certificate-based authentication maps to a user account. When this is enabled, SSH will not require local user association with an authorized-key and instead enforces that the username used to log in is present within the certificate. The SSH server will check for the username in certificate fields Common Name or User Principle Name for a match. If a certificate is not used for authentication then this configuration has no effect on SSH authentication. The no form of this command disables the SSH enforcement of username in the certificate. Syntax:
- no ssh certificate-as-authorized-key
- ssh certificate-as-authorized-key 3398.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vlan <VLAN-LIST> command do?

The vlan <VLAN-LIST> command creates a VLAN and changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. By default, the VLAN is enabled. To disable a VLAN, use the shutdown command. Public vlan (interface) 3529 If the specified VLAN exists, this command changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. If a range of VLANs is specified, the context does not change. NOTE VLANs used for internal purposes using the command system internal vlan range cannot be used for any other (L2) purposes. The no form of this command removes a VLAN. VLAN 1 is the default VLAN and cannot be deleted. Specifies a single ID, or a series of IDs separated by commas (2, 3, 4), dashes (2-4), or both (2-4, 6). Range: 1 to 4094. A m aximum of 2048 (5420, 6200 Switch Series) VLANs are suppor ted. Syntax: vlan access <VLAN-ID>.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show interface vxlan vteps command do?

The show interface vxlan vteps command displays detailed VTEP information for the VXLAN interface. VTEP states can be one of the following: • operational: Tunnel endpoint is fully programmed on the switch hardware. • configuration_error: Tunnel endpoint programming in the switch hardware failed due to misconfiguration. • no_hw_resources: Tunnel endpoint programming failed in the switch hardware due to insufficient resources. • activating: Initial state of tunnel endpoint when it is configured. When a tunnel endpoint is a directly connected via nexthop, then nexthop reachability appears empty (--). Show detail VTEP information. Specifies the IP address of a VTEP peer in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Syntax: show interface vxlan vteps [detail | <IP-ADDR>].

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erase all zeroize command do?

The erase all zeroize command restores the switch to its factory default configuration. You will be prompted before the procedure starts. Once complete, the switch will restart from the primary image with factory default settings. Syntax: erase [all] zeroize.
